title: Mechanical Vibrations and Oscillators
subtitle: The physics payoff of second-order theory: springs, damping regimes, resonance, and coupled oscillation.
description: The physics payoff of second-order theory: springs, damping regimes, resonance, and coupled oscillation.

atAGlance.title: Unit 11 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: This is the physics payoff of second-order theory. A mass on a spring obeys $m x'' + c x' + k x = F(t)$, and every behavior in the unit --- pure oscillation, decay, blow-up, and shared rhythm --- is read off that one equation by inspecting its mass, damping, stiffness, and forcing.

bullets.body: Hooke's law $F=-kx$ and the natural frequency $\omega_0=\sqrt{k/m}$.
$x = C\cos(\omega_0 t - \phi)$ and the amplitude-phase form.
the discriminant $c^2-4mk$ sorts the motion into over-, critically, and underdamped.
driving at $\omega_0$ produces unbounded growth; driving near it produces beats.
eigenvalues of $\mathbf{x}''=A\mathbf{x}$ give the normal-mode frequencies.
one equation, $x''+\omega_0^2 x=0$, solved four ways and hiding inside almost every stable system.

modules.title: Simple Harmonic Motion and Hooke's Law
modules.topics: A spring resists displacement with a force proportional to how far it is stretched and directed back toward equilibrium. That single linear law turns Newton's second law into the master oscillator equation.

formulas.title: Hooke's Law and the Natural Frequency
formulas.body:
\[ F = -kx, \qquad m x'' + k x = 0, \qquad x'' + \omega_0^2 x = 0, \qquad \omega_0 = \sqrt{\tfrac{k}{m}}. \]
The period and ordinary frequency follow directly from $\omega_0$:
\[ T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}, \qquad f = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi}. \]
A hanging mass settles where gravity balances the spring, $mg = kL$, which is how a
measured static stretch $L$ delivers the stiffness $k = mg/L$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Angular Frequency Is Not Frequency
warning.body:
The $\omega_0$ inside $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is the \strong{angular} frequency in
radians per second; the ordinary frequency $f$ in cycles per second is smaller by a
factor of $2\pi$, $f=\omega_0/(2\pi)$. A second trap lives one step earlier: after
dividing by the mass you read off $\omega_0^{2}$, \emph{not} $\omega_0$. For
$2x''+18x=0$ the equation becomes $x''+9x=0$, so $\omega_0^{2}=9$ and
$\omega_0=3$ --- never $9$.

modules.title: Free Undamped Vibrations
modules.topics: With no damping and no forcing, the imaginary roots $\pm i\omega_0$ give a clean, everlasting sinusoid. Two initial conditions --- position and velocity --- pin down its two constants.

formulas.title: General Solution and Amplitude-Phase Form
formulas.body:
\[ x(t) = c_1 \cos(\omega_0 t) + c_2 \sin(\omega_0 t) = C\cos(\omega_0 t - \phi), \]
\[ C = \sqrt{c_1^{2} + c_2^{2}}, \qquad \tan\phi = \frac{c_2}{c_1}. \]
The amplitude $C$ and phase $\phi$ depend on the initial data, while $\omega_0$ is
fixed by the physical system alone --- a stiffer spring (larger $k$) or a lighter
mass raises $\omega_0$ and the motion runs faster.

methodology.title: Solving a Spring-Mass IVP
steps: Divide by the mass to reach $x'' + \omega_0^{2}x = 0$ and read $\omega_0$.
Apply $x(0)$ to fix $c_1$; differentiate and apply $x'(0)$ to fix $c_2$ (remember $x'(0) = \omega_0 c_2$).
Collapse to $C\cos(\omega_0 t - \phi)$ with $C=\sqrt{c_1^2+c_2^2}$ to read the amplitude.

modules.title: Damped Vibrations and the Damping Discriminant
modules.topics: Add a dashpot and the equation gains a velocity term $c x'$. The sign of one discriminant decides the entire character of the motion.

formulas.title: The Damping Discriminant
formulas.body:
\[ m x'' + c x' + k x = 0, \qquad \Delta = c^{2} - 4mk. \]
\[
\begin{array}{l|l|l}
\strong{Sign} & \strong{Roots} & \strong{Regime}\\ \hline
\Delta > 0 & \text{two distinct real} & \text{overdamped (no oscillation)}\\
\Delta = 0 & \text{repeated real} & \text{critically damped}\\
\Delta < 0 & \text{complex } -b \pm i\mu & \text{underdamped (decaying oscillation)}
\end{array}
\]
The critical value $c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ is the boundary; it returns the system to
equilibrium fastest \emph{without} overshooting.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Quasi-Frequency Is Not $\omega_0$
warning.body:
An underdamped solution $e^{-bt}(c_1\cos\mu t + c_2\sin\mu t)$ oscillates at the
\strong{quasi-frequency} $\mu = \sqrt{\omega_0^{2}-b^{2}}$, which is strictly
\emph{below} the undamped natural frequency $\omega_0$. Damping always slows the
oscillation; it never speeds it up.

modules.title: Forced Vibrations, Beats, and Resonance
modules.topics: Drive the system with $F_0\cos(\omega t)$ and the steady response depends sharply on how the drive frequency $\omega$ compares to the natural frequency $\omega_0$.

formulas.title: Steady Amplitude, Beats, and Resonance
formulas.body:
For undamped forced motion $m x'' + k x = F_0\cos(\omega t)$ with $\omega\neq\omega_0$,
\[ x_p(t) = \frac{F_0}{m(\omega_0^{2}-\omega^{2})}\cos(\omega t). \]
As $\omega \to \omega_0$ the denominator vanishes and the amplitude blows up. At
exact \strong{resonance} $\omega=\omega_0$ the trial gains a factor of $t$,
\[ x_p(t) = t\,(A\cos\omega_0 t + B\sin\omega_0 t), \]
so the amplitude grows linearly without bound. For $\omega$ \emph{close to but not
equal} to $\omega_0$ the two nearby frequencies interfere to produce \strong{beats}
--- a fast oscillation under a slow envelope whose period lengthens as
$\omega\to\omega_0$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Resonance Versus Beats
warning.body:
Beats and resonance are not the same phenomenon. \strong{Beats} need
$\omega \approx \omega_0$ (close, not equal) and give a \emph{bounded}, slowly
modulated amplitude. \strong{Resonance} needs $\omega = \omega_0$ exactly and gives
\emph{unbounded} growth from the resonant $t$ factor. Mistaking one for the other
misreads whether a structure merely throbs or is being torn apart.

modules.title: Coupled Oscillators and Normal Modes
modules.topics: When two masses share a spring, neither equation stands alone: each acceleration depends on the other's position. Writing the system as $\mathbf{x}'' = A\mathbf{x}$ turns the physics into an eigenvalue problem.

formulas.title: Normal Modes from Eigenvalues
formulas.body:
\[ \mathbf{x}'' = A\,\mathbf{x}, \qquad A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}, \qquad
   \omega^{2} = -\lambda, \qquad \omega = \sqrt{-\lambda}. \]
Each eigenvalue gives one \strong{normal mode}: a motion in which all masses
oscillate at a single shared frequency with a fixed amplitude ratio (the
eigenvector). A system with $n$ degrees of freedom has exactly $n$ modes, and the
general motion is a \strong{superposition} of them, each with its own amplitude and
phase.

methodology.title: Finding the Normal Modes
steps: Assemble the coupling matrix $A$ from the equations of motion.
Convert each to a mode frequency $\omega = \sqrt{-\lambda}$.
Find each eigenvector for the mode shape (amplitude ratio), then superpose.

modules.title: The Harmonic Oscillator Solved Four Ways
modules.topics: Strip away damping and forcing and one equation remains, $x''+\omega_0^{2}x=0$. It is worth meeting from several directions because it is the linear heartbeat of nearly every stable system.

formulas.title: One Equation, Many Faces
formulas.body:
\[ x'' + \omega_0^{2}x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; r^{2}+\omega_0^{2}=0 \;\Longrightarrow\;
   r = \pm i\omega_0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = c_1\cos\omega_0 t + c_2\sin\omega_0 t. \]
The energy method --- multiply by $x'$ and integrate --- yields the conserved
quantity
\[ \tfrac{1}{2}(x')^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_0^{2}x^{2} = \text{const}. \]
The same equation governs an ideal $LC$ circuit $L q'' + \tfrac{1}{C}q = 0$ with
$\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, because near any stable equilibrium a smooth potential
looks quadratic and its restoring force looks linear.

concept.title: Unit 11 Key Takeaway
concept.body:
Everything flows from $m x'' + c x' + k x = F(t)$. The
\highlight{natural frequency $\omega_0=\sqrt{k/m}$} sets the rhythm;
the \highlight{discriminant $c^2-4mk$} sorts damped motion into
over-, critically, and underdamped; \highlight{resonance at
$\omega=\omega_0$} forces a $t$ factor and unbounded growth while a near-match gives
\highlight{beats}; and the \highlight{eigenvalues of
$\mathbf{x}''=A\mathbf{x}$} unlock the normal modes of coupled motion. Underneath it
all sits the \highlight{harmonic oscillator $x''+\omega_0^2 x=0$}, the
universal linear model of a system near stable equilibrium.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Mechanical Vibrations and Oscillators

takeaway.title: Unit 11 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Read the motion straight off the coefficients. The
\highlight{natural frequency $\omega_0=\sqrt{k/m}$} and the collapse
to \highlight{$C\cos(\omega_0 t-\phi)$} govern free motion; the
\highlight{discriminant $c^2-4mk$} sorts damping into
\highlight{overdamped ($>0$)}, \highlight{critically
damped ($=0$, $c=2\sqrt{mk}$)}, and \highlight{underdamped ($<0$)};
\highlight{resonance at $\omega=\omega_0$} injects the $t$ factor that
drives $x_p=\tfrac{t}{10}\sin 5t$ to grow without bound; and the
\highlight{eigenvalues of $\mathbf{x}''=A\mathbf{x}$} deliver the
normal-mode frequencies $\omega=\sqrt{-\lambda}$ whose superposition is the full
coupled motion.
